lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying printing inks, inkjet inks, and inkjet cartridges to external converters, printers, packaging producers, and other commercial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive ink rooms, distributors without manufacturing, printers whose ink making is incidental, consumer retail-only resellers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B product sales and technical-service relationships, typically priced by weight, unit, formula, or program with revenue shaped by volumes, pigments and solvents, performance specifications, color matching, regulatory requirements, and customer qualification.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for broad chemical manufacturing (3251/2/3/9), while O*NET describes mixing/blending tasks across industries rather than wage-weighted 325910 work. I map formula retrieval, batch records, color-data analysis, scheduling, purchasing, compliance, customer support, and administration to exposed tasks but leave physical charging, milling, tinting, sampling, cleaning, packaging, maintenance, and release accountability largely unexposed. Aggregation may misstate both technical-service and shop-floor shares.
a.rationale: Ink production has information-rich formulation, matching, quality, and customer-service work, yet EPA describes a physical batch process of mixing, grinding or milling, tinting, thinning, packaging, and cleaning. AI can assist decisions and records more readily than substitute for hands-on production.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll and time-on-task study covering formulation/color labs, batch operations, quality, technical service, sales, scheduling, procurement, compliance, maintenance, warehousing, and administration.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix was found.
The estimate excludes conventional non-AI equipment automation already embedded in production.

rho.bridge: Census measures AI use in any function across US businesses and NIST provides manufacturing-wide use cases and barriers, not five-year implementation in ink plants. I translate those data to exposed ink workflows; small firms' legacy equipment and limited labeled color/process data likely make national and large-firm diffusion evidence upward-biased, while bounded document and forecasting tools are comparatively accessible.
rho.rationale: Forecasting, formula search, color correction suggestions, batch-record review, anomaly detection, maintenance, and technical-document retrieval can be deployed incrementally. Customer validation, physical variability, legacy systems, cybersecurity, VOC and food-contact concerns, and human quality decisions cap implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Plant audits and vendor-verified pre/post labor and quality outcomes for LMM ink makers using formulation, color-management, eQMS, predictive-maintenance, scheduling, and document AI.
caveats: General AI adoption does not equal labor substitution.
Manufacturing examples are not representative controlled studies.

e.rationale: The NAICS category is narrowly defined around ink manufacturing and most operating firms should supply repeat external demand. Ineligibility remains for captive or incidental operations, firms without standalone control economics, and misclassified distributors or cartridge resellers.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 325910 roster matched to production assets, external-customer invoices, ownership, standalone financials, and normalized EBITDA.
caveats: The injected firm count is estimated and may not map cleanly from establishments to transferable firms.
Inkjet cartridge makers may have a different model from bulk commercial-ink formulators.

s5.rationale: A five-year transfer probability in the low twenties is plausible for private specialty-chemical manufacturers, but no public source measures qualifying control transfers for 325910. Succession and strategic consolidation support turnover, while customer dependence and environmental liabilities may deter buyers.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and specialist-advisor deal records reconciled to the eligible roster, with owner age, succession plans, and exclusion of minority or internal transactions.
caveats: Business dynamics measure births, deaths, and firm age rather than control transfers.
Plant closures and acquisitions of printer ink rooms are not necessarily qualifying firm transfers.

q.rationale: Product pricing lets suppliers initially retain efficiency gains. Contract renewals, raw-material indices, competitive bids, and large converters' purchasing power share savings, while custom color, press performance, food-contact work, technical service, and qualification costs protect a meaningful portion.
q.change_evidence: Customer agreements and invoice cohorts showing indexation, open-book terms, productivity clauses, rebid frequency, qualification cost, switching events, and margin retention after prior process improvements.
caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Pigment, resin, solvent, and energy volatility can dominate observed pricing.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for printing customers and broader 3251/2/3/9 chemical manufacturing, while Census e-commerce and USPS mail data indicate opposing packaging and publication channels; none measures constant-quality 325910 demand. I combine 0.6% annual projected printing-output growth, 1.0% broader chemical growth, 9.8% recent e-commerce growth, and a 3.3% USPS volume decline into a near-flat five-year ink-demand judgment. E-commerce may overstate packaging-ink growth and USPS may overstate total publication decline.
d5.rationale: Packaging, labels, digital and inkjet applications support demand, while digitization continues to erode transactional, publication, and some commercial print. The industry mix determines whether modest packaging growth outweighs declining print intensity.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price 325910 shipments by application and technology—flexographic, gravure, offset, screen, digital, packaging, label, publication, and cartridge—plus customer volume forecasts.
caveats: End-market proxies do not translate one-for-one into ink volume or value.
Ink technology shifts can change ink consumption per printed unit.

o.rationale: Remaining printed output requires physical ink formulation, manufacture, quality assurance, and delivery. Software can optimize formulation and workflows but cannot supply the material; displacement comes mainly from printer insourcing, imports, integrated suppliers, or technologies that use different consumables.
o.change_evidence: Printer and converter procurement interviews quantifying ink-room insourcing, supplier consolidation, imports, cartridge substitution, and adoption of technologies that materially reduce external ink requirements.
caveats: Operator requirement is inferred rather than observed as a market-share statistic.
Captive ink kitchens and digital OEM consumables may shift who performs the operator role.

narrative.executive_view: Printing ink is a repeat B2B consumable with technical switching costs, but demand mixes growing packaging and labels with structurally challenged publication and commercial print. The labor opportunity is concentrated in formulation, records, quality analytics, planning, and customer workflows rather than the physical batch process.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve formula retrieval, color-matching recommendations, batch and deviation review, demand planning, maintenance signals, compliance search, and technical service. Operators still charge materials, run mills and mixers, tint, sample, clean, package, troubleshoot, and approve output.
narrative.value_capture: Custom performance, press compatibility, food-contact requirements, color consistency, and technical support create retention. Large converters and contract indexation still expose savings to rebids and customer negotiation.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied 53-firm estimate suggests a usable but finite pool. The narrow NAICS definition supports eligibility, yet captive operations, cartridge-focused models, standalone economics, environmental liabilities, and ownership must be checked firm by firm.
narrative.demand_durability: Packaging, labels, and e-commerce logistics are supportive, while mail and publication volumes continue to decline. BLS nevertheless projects slight real output growth for printing, so the base case is near-flat to modest growth rather than wholesale disappearance.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Application mix, customer concentration, raw-material volatility, solvent and wastewater obligations, food-contact requirements, and legacy systems are material. Public evidence does not directly measure six-digit task weights, transfers, contracts, or constant-quality ink volume.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat consumable demand plus formulation and press-performance switching costs support customer persistence and operational improvement.
narrative.principal_weakness: Structural decline in publication and commercial print can overwhelm packaging growth for firms with the wrong end-market mix.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 325910: Printing Ink Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the industry as manufacturing printing and inkjet inks and inkjet cartridges.

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects printing and related support output from $67.0 billion in 2024 to $71.1 billion in 2034, a 0.6% annual rate, and broader 3251/2/3/9 chemical output at 1.0% annually.

sources.title: O*NET: Mixing and Blending Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders
sources.supports: Names Ink Blender as a reported title and lists formula reading, weighing, mixing, monitoring, recording, sampling, cleaning, maintenance, and testing tasks.

sources.title: Ink Formulating Effluent Guidelines
sources.supports: Describes oil- and water-base ink batch production through mixing, grinding or milling, tinting, thinning, packaging, shipment, and tank cleaning; identifies covered facilities with NAICS 325910.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports Census BTOS business AI use around 17-20% in Dec. 2025-May 2026 and higher use among firms with at least 100 employees.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing
sources.supports: Identifies predictive maintenance, quality control, and forecasting uses and implementation barriers in data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration.

sources.title: Monitoring Information by Industry: Printing and Publishing
sources.supports: Explains that ink solvents can be VOCs or hazardous air pollutants and describes compliant low-VOC/HAP inks and capture/control approaches.

sources.title: Hazard Identification of Printing Ink Substances Used on Exterior Food Packaging
sources.supports: States that some photoinitiators in UV-cured exterior food-packaging inks may migrate to food and describes hazard identification for about 100 photoinitiators.

sources.title: About Business Dynamics Statistics
sources.supports: Explains that BDS measures openings, closings, startups, shutdowns, job flows, firm age, and size, but not qualifying control transfers.

sources.title: Quarterly Retail E-Commerce Sales, First Quarter 2026
sources.supports: Reports Q1 2026 US e-commerce sales of $326.7 billion, up 9.8% year over year and 16.9% of total retail sales.

sources.title: USPS Issues Report to Congress for 2025
sources.supports: Reports total mail and package volume decreased 3.3% in fiscal 2025 from fiscal 2024.
